confirmed.title: iOS fast-path for already-downloaded models skips the C3 integrity gate (magic + SHA-256) that the fresh-download path enforces
confirmed.symptom: On relaunch, if a model file is already present at destination, OnboardingModel.install() skips straight to engine.load() without ever calling ModelIntegrity.verify — no GGUF magic check, no SHA-256 comparison against the catalog-pinned hash.
confirmed.rootCause: `if !FileManager.default.fileExists(atPath: destination.path) { ...download... }` only gates the download step; there is no re-verification of a file that already exists at `destination` before `engine.load(model:at:)` is called at line 125. Both ModelDownloader.swift (ChunkedDownloadDelegate, lines 131-145) and BackgroundModelDownloader.swift (lines 145-164) DO call ModelIntegrity.verify right after a completed download and delete the file on failure — but if the app process is killed in the crash window between `moveItem(at:to:)` succeeding and `ModelIntegrity.verify` completing (a real window: SHA-256 over a multi-GB file takes real wall-clock time, and iOS can jetsam/kill an app anytime), the file is left sitting at `destination` fully un-verified. The next launch's `fileExists` check treats it as good and loads it directly. This is precisely the class of gap this review was told to watch for: Android's ModelDownloadEngine.download() (just fixed this session) re-verifies GGUF magic AND SHA-256 on its 'already exists' fast-path (ModelDownloadEngine.kt:84-92) before trusting a pre-existing final file — iOS's equivalent fast-path in OnboardingModel.install() has no such check at all.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In OnboardingModel.install(), when `destination` already exists, run the same ModelIntegrity.verify(fileURL:expectedSHA256:) check (using ModelCatalog.entry(id: model.id)?.sha256) before proceeding to engine.load(); on failure, delete the file and fall through to a fresh download, mirroring Android's ModelDownloadEngine.download() fast-path.
confirmed.failureScenario: User is mid-onboarding, iOS kills the app (memory pressure / user force-quit) in the ~1-5s window after the multi-GB file is moved to its final destination but before SHA-256 verification finishes. On next launch, the app finds the file present, skips the download, and hands a never-verified (potentially truncated/corrupted/MITM'd) GGUF straight to the inference engine.
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed by direct read. OnboardingModel.swift:101 checks FileManager.default.fileExists(atPath:) and if true, skips the entire block (lines 102-121) that contains the only path to ModelIntegrity.verify() — which is called exclusively inside the download-completion delegates (ChunkedDownloadDelegate.urlSession(didCompleteWithError:) at ModelDownloader.swift:138, and BackgroundModelDownloader's URLSessionDownloadDelegate at BackgroundModelDownloader.swift:153). There is no standalone/reusable verify call anywhere else in install() or in ModelManager — the fast path falls straight through to engine.load() at line 125. This is precisely the cross-platform parity gap the task describes: Android's ModelDownloadEngine.download() (ModelDownloadEngine.kt:84-92) was fixed this session to re-run the GGUF-magic + SHA-256 gate on its already-exists fast path before trusting/reusing the file (and truncates + falls through to a real download on mismatch), but iOS's equivalent fast path has no analogous re-verification at all. The failure scenario (app killed mid-onboarding after file move but before/without verification, then relaunched) is concrete and reachable — FileManager.fileExists only tests presence, not validity, and nothing else in the reviewed files re-checks integrity for a pre-existing file before load.
confirmed.reproduction: 1. Fresh install, start onboarding, download begins to `destination`. 2. Suppose write-time corruption, a manually placed/partial file, or (more realistically per the documented threat model) any pre-existing file at `destination` that happens to have valid bytes at some point but was never freshly downloaded+verified in this run (e.g. copied in by a restore/backup, or present from a previous run that crashed after moveItem but is now stale/tampered). 3. User relaunches, install(model) runs: FileManager.default.fileExists(atPath: destination.path) == true → skips lines 102-121 entirely, jumps straight to `engine.load(model:at:)` with zero calls to ModelIntegrity.verify. A corrupted/mismatched-hash file is loaded straight into llama.cpp with no gate.
confirmed.change: In OnboardingModel.swift install(), before the existing 'download if missing' gate, added a re-verification step: if a file already exists at `destination`, run the same ModelIntegrity.verify(fileURL:expectedSHA256:) gate the fresh-download path enforces (using ModelCatalog.entry(id: model.id)?.sha256). On failure (or thrown error, via try?) the file is deleted so control falls through into the existing '!fileExists' branch and triggers a real re-download. This closes the crash-window gap where a file could be moved into place but the process killed before the multi-GB SHA-256 check finished, leaving an unverified file that a naive fileExists() check would trust on the next launch.
confirmed.verified: Read ModelIntegrity.swift to confirm verify(fileURL:expectedSHA256:) throws ModelIntegrityError on bad magic/checksum mismatch, matching the pattern already used in ModelDownloader.swift's ChunkedDownloadDelegate and BackgroundModelDownloader.swift's didFinishDownloadingTo. Ran `cd apple/QuenderinKit && swift build` twice (before finalizing and again at the end) — both times: 'Build complete!' with zero errors/warnings.

confirmed.title: iOS decode loop collapses recoverable KV-cache-full (rc==1) into fatal, unlike Android's shared loop which already fixed this
confirmed.symptom: On iOS, a long chat that fills the KV cache mid-generation (or a reused-prefix prefill that no longer fits) throws InferenceError.generationFailed and drops the reply entirely (partial tokens already yielded to the stream are followed by a thrown error, not a clean finish), instead of gracefully stopping/reprefilling like Android does.
confirmed.rootCause: The Swift `decode(_:)` helper (LlamaEngine.swift:227-231) collapses `llama_decode`'s return code to a plain Bool via `== 0`, so it cannot distinguish `rc == 1` ("no free KV slot — cache full, recoverable") from a genuine negative/fatal error. Both the prefill call (line 242) and the mid-generation feedback call (line 269) treat any non-zero rc identically as fatal and call `continuation.finish(throwing:)`. Compare this to the Android/C++ shared loop in llama_generate.h:42-51,83-96,126-132, whose own header comment explicitly documents that "Treating 1 as fatal (the original bug here) silently blanks or truncates replies the moment a long chat fills the context" — i.e. this exact bug class was found and fixed for Android's generateWithKVReuse, but the equivalent fix was never ported to the Swift twin, which the file's own doc comment (LlamaEngine.swift lines 10-11, 233-236) states is supposed to mirror the same KV-reuse fail-safe behavior.
confirmed.suggestedFix: In `decode(_:)` (or its two call sites), return/propagate the raw llama_decode return code instead of collapsing to Bool. At prefill: if rc==1 and a KV-reuse prefix was in play, clear the cache, drop reuse, and retry a full reprefill once (mirroring llama_generate.h:83-90) before treating it as fatal. At mid-generation: if rc==1, treat it as a graceful stop (`continuation.finish()` with whatever was already yielded) rather than `finish(throwing:)`, only treating a genuinely negative rc with zero tokens produced so far as fatal (mirroring llama_generate.h:126-132).
confirmed.failureScenario: A user has a long-running chat that approaches n_ctx. On Android, the reply gracefully truncates and the user sees a complete (if shorter) response, with the next turn cleanly reprefilling. On iOS with the identical conversation length, the exact same reply throws a hard error instead — the user sees a failure/blank turn where Android would have silently succeeded with partial output, an inconsistent and worse experience for no functional reason.
confirmed.reasoning: iOS LlamaEngine.swift's `decode()` closure (line 227-231) collapses the llama_decode return code to a plain Bool via `== 0`, discarding the distinction between rc==1 (recoverable, KV-cache-full — per the shared C++ header's own explicit contract comment at llama_generate.h:42-51) and negative (genuinely fatal) codes. Both the prefill-decode failure (line 242-245) and the mid-generation feed-back decode failure (line 269-272) unconditionally call `continuation.finish(throwing: InferenceError.generationFailed(...))`, with no reprefill-without-reuse retry and no graceful-stop-on-cache-full path. This is reachable: any long chat whose KV cache fills exactly during the mid-generation feed-back decode, or during a reuse-prefill that no longer fits after eviction, hits `frc != 0` on iOS and throws — even after tokens were already yielded via `continuation.yield(piece)` on line 265, so a partially-generated reply is discarded rather than gracefully finished. `KVCacheReuse.swift` (read in full) is a pure prefix-matching heuristic with no cache-full avoidance, so nothing upstream prevents rc==1 from occurring. Android's twin `generateWithKVReuse` (llama_generate.h:83-96, 126-132) explicitly special-cases rc==1 both at prefill (drop-reuse-and-retry) and mid-generation (graceful stop, keep partial output) — confirming this is a real, already-fixed-on-one-platform bug missing on iOS.
confirmed.reproduction: Long iOS chat approaches n_ctx; either (a) the reused-prefix prefill decode returns 1 because the full turn no longer fits after cache growth, or (b) a mid-generation feed-back decode (line 269) returns 1 because the KV cache filled during this reply's own token generation. In both cases iOS's `decode()` returns false and the caller throws InferenceError.generationFailed, discarding/erroring the turn instead of Android's graceful reprefill-once (prefill case) or stop-and-keep-partial-output (mid-gen case).
confirmed.change: In apple/QuenderinKit/Sources/QuenderinKit/LlamaEngine.swift's runGeneration: changed the nested `decode(_:)` helper to return the raw Int32 llama_decode return code instead of collapsing it to Bool. At the prefill call site (~line 245-260): if rc==1 and the KV-reuse plan had decodeFrom>0 (a reused prefix was in play), clear the cache/mirror and retry a full reprefill once before treating any remaining non-zero rc as fatal (mirrors llama_generate.h:83-96). At the mid-generation feedback call site (~line 279-295): captured `producedBefore` (the yield count before this token) so a code==1 mid-stream is now a graceful `continuation.finish()` (loop `break`) instead of `finish(throwing:)`, and only a genuinely negative rc with nothing yet produced (producedBefore==0) throws (mirrors llama_generate.h:126-132). Also caught and fixed a self-introduced bug during the edit: the first attempt checked `produced == 0` AFTER `produced += 1` had already run, which is always false (dead code) — fixed by capturing `producedBefore` prior to the increment.
confirmed.verified: Manually re-read the edited region (LlamaEngine.swift lines 223-298) line-by-line for type/logic correctness (Int32 returns, plan.decodeFrom is Int per KVCacheReuse.swift, comparisons all valid). Ran `swift build` in apple/QuenderinKit — 'Build complete!' with no errors. Note: the edited code lives inside `#if canImport(llama)`, which is false in this environment (no llama.xcframework/QUENDERIN_LLAMA_DIR linked), so the block is compiled OUT of this particular `swift build` and was not type-checked by the compiler here; verification for that block is manual read-through only, consistent with the task's allowance for JNI/native code that can't be built in this environment. The surrounding actor code (outside the #if) does compile and confirms no syntax damage to the file as a whole.

confirmed.title: Android's regex-based JSON key extraction ignores nesting; iOS's JSONSerialization only reads top-level keys — parser can diverge on which tool/answer is selected
confirmed.symptom: For the exact same planner output text, Android's AgentDecisionParser.parse() can select a completely different AgentDecision than iOS's — either fabricating a FinalAnswer from a nested field, or invoking a tool that iOS would refuse to run at all (PLAN_ERROR).
confirmed.rootCause: extractString(json, key) at AgentDecision.kt:55-59 uses a flat regex `"$key"\s*:\s*"..."` over the whole brace-balanced substring returned by firstJsonObject, with no notion of JSON nesting/object depth. It will match a key that appears anywhere inside the text — including inside a nested object value — not just at the top level of the decision object. iOS's AgentDecisionParser.parse() (AgentDecision.swift:14-27) instead runs the text through JSONSerialization.jsonObject and only ever reads object["answer"] / object["tool"] / object["input"] at the top level, so nested occurrences of those keys are invisible to it. Verified by direct execution: for input `{"tool":"calculator","input":{"nested":"x"},"extra":{"answer":"nested value"}}`, compiled Kotlin extractString(json,"answer") returns "nested value" (Android would return FinalAnswer("nested value")), while the equivalent Swift JSONSerialization lookup returns object["answer"] == nil, object["tool"] == "calculator" (iOS returns .useTool("calculator", ...)). For input `{"thought":{"tool":"delete","input":"all files"},"other":"x"}`, Kotlin's extractString finds tool="delete", input="all files" from the nested field and Android would attempt AgentDecision.UseTool("delete","all files") (subject to the SafetyBlocklist keyword check, which happens to catch "delete" here, but would NOT catch an innocuous-named nested tool like "echo"), while Swift's JSONSerialization sees object["tool"] == nil at the top level and the whole parse returns nil, which AgentLoop.run() treats as HaltReason.PLAN_ERROR — no tool executes on iOS.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Parse the JSON structurally on Android (e.g. org.json.JSONObject, which ships in the Android SDK, or a minimal dependency-free JSON parser that respects nesting) instead of a flat regex scan, so only true top-level "answer"/"tool"/"input" keys are read — matching iOS's JSONSerialization semantics. At minimum, extractString should stop matching keys once it descends into a nested `{` it didn't open at depth 0.
confirmed.failureScenario: A local on-device model (especially a smaller/quantized one that imitates the few-shot schema imperfectly, or echoes a "thought"/"reasoning" scratch field before its real decision) emits JSON where the true tool/answer keys the model intended are wrapped inside another object, e.g. `{"reasoning":{"tool":"units","input":"..."},"final":true}`. Android silently invokes the `units` tool via the nested keys and continues the loop; iOS returns PLAN_ERROR and shows the user 'The agent couldn't turn this goal into a valid plan.' Two platforms shipping 'the same feature' now behave differently for identical model output — Android executes a tool call the user-facing iOS build would have rejected outright, contradicting the project's explicit cross-platform-parity contract for this exact file (see the H13 comment in the same file claiming 'parity with Swift').
confirmed.reasoning: Concretely reproducible with a Python/Swift test harness. Android's extractString() regex `"$key"\s*:\s*"..."` searches the ENTIRE extracted JSON substring (not scoped to top-level), so for input `{"reasoning":{"tool":"units","input":"2 miles to km"},"final":true}`, `extractString(json,"answer")` returns null (no answer key anywhere) but `extractString(json,"tool")` matches the nested `"tool":"units"` and returns `UseTool("units", "2 miles to km")` via AgentLoop.kt:55's parse() call succeeding. Verified this behavior directly with a Python regex reproducing the exact Kotlin pattern. Meanwhile iOS's JSONSerialization on the identical string only sees top-level keys {"final", "reasoning"} (verified by running actual Swift code) -- object["answer"] and object["tool"] are both nil, so parse() returns nil, and AgentLoop.swift:68's `guard let decision = ... else` fires, returning haltReason .planError. This is a real, concrete, reachable divergence for identical planner text: Android silently executes a tool call that iOS refuses. Nothing in the reviewed files guards against it -- the firstJsonObject/firstJSONObject brace-walking (the H13 fix already documented in both files) only prevents merging a SECOND top-level JSON object; it does nothing to scope key-extraction to top-level within a single object, so the nested-key regex issue is a distinct, unaddressed gap. The file's own comment claims 'parity with Swift' for the H13 fix, but that parity claim does not extend to nesting, so this is not the documented/intentional tradeoff -- it's an unaddressed gap in an otherwise deliberately-hardened parser.
confirmed.reproduction: Feed both parsers the string `{"reasoning":{"tool":"units","input":"2 miles to km"},"final":true}`. Android AgentDecisionParser.parse() returns AgentDecision.UseTool("units","2 miles to km") (verified via regex simulation in Python matching Kotlin's exact pattern). iOS AgentDecisionParser.parse() returns nil (verified by executing real Swift/JSONSerialization code), which AgentLoop.swift:68 turns into HaltReason.planError. Confirmed AgentLoop.kt:55 uses the Kotlin parser's return value directly to decide between continuing the tool-use loop and PLAN_ERROR, so the divergence is reachable in the actual control flow, not just the parser in isolation.
confirmed.change: In android/quenderin-core/src/main/kotlin/ai/quenderin/core/AgentDecision.kt, replaced the flat, nesting-blind regex in `extractString(json, key)` (previously `Regex("\"$key\"\s*:\s*\"((?:[^\"\\]|\\.)*)\"").find(json)`) with a depth-aware scan that walks the string the same way `firstJsonObject` already does (tracking brace/bracket depth and skipping over quoted-string contents), and only recognizes a `"key": "value"` match when it occurs at depth == 1 (i.e. a direct top-level member of the outer object). Keys appearing inside a nested object or array (depth > 1) are now invisible to the parser, matching iOS's `JSONSerialization`-based top-level-only read in AgentDecision.swift. No other function or file was touched; iOS's AgentDecision.swift was already correct and required no change. Also added two new checks to android/quenderin-core/src/verify/CoreVerify.kt (`parity: decision parser ignores keys nested inside another object (no fabricated answer)` and `parity: decision parser returns nil when tool/answer only appear nested (matches iOS PLAN_ERROR)`) using the exact repro inputs from the bug report, placed next to the existing H13 parity check in the same style/comment convention (`// parity:<tag>` trailing comments).
confirmed.verified: Compiled with `cd /Users/s_avelova/Documents/projects/quenderin/android/quenderin-core && kotlinc src/main/kotlin/ai/quenderin/core/*.kt src/verify/CoreVerify.kt -include-runtime -d /tmp/core_verify_agent-safety.jar` (zero errors/warnings). Ran `java -jar /tmp/core_verify_agent-safety.jar`: output ends with `ALL PASSED`, `grep -c "^  FAIL"` on the full output returns 0. Confirmed the two new checks explicitly print `ok`: `ok   parity: decision parser ignores keys nested inside another object (no fabricated answer)` and `ok   parity: decision parser returns nil when tool/answer only appear nested (matches iOS PLAN_ERROR)` — reproducing both exact inputs from the bug report (`{"tool":"calculator","input":{"nested":"x"},"extra":{"answer":"nested value"}}` now correctly yields `tool:calculator` instead of the fabricated `answer:nested value`; `{"thought":{"tool":"delete","input":"all files"},"other":"x"}` now correctly yields `nil`, matching iOS's PLAN_ERROR instead of Android previously invoking the nested tool). Also confirmed all pre-existing parity/decision/blocklist/agent-loop checks still pass (no regression), including the H13 first-object, unicode-escape, and short-escape decision-parser checks.

confirmed.title: Chat/Agent in-flight work silently cancelled on tab switch (Android only) — iOS TabView keeps tabs alive, Android's single-slot `when` tears them down
confirmed.symptom: User sends a chat message or runs an agent goal, then taps another bottom-nav tab before the response finishes; when they tap back to Chat/Agent, the response never arrives, the transcript looks like the message was never answered (silently reverts to whatever was last persisted), and any unsent draft text in the input field is gone.
confirmed.rootCause: MainTabs.kt renders exactly one tab's composable subtree at a time via `when (tab) { 0 -> ChatScreen(...); 1 -> AgentScreen(...); else -> SettingsScreen(...) }` (lines 63-67). ChatScreen.kt and AgentScreen.kt each call `rememberCoroutineScope()` (ChatScreen.kt:67, AgentScreen.kt:61) and launch the send/run coroutine on `Dispatchers.IO` from that scope (ChatScreen.kt:158 `chat.send(text)`, AgentScreen.kt:171 `session.run(g)`). A `rememberCoroutineScope()` is tied to the composable's slot in the composition; when the `when` branch switches away, that whole subtree (and its remembered scope + ConversationCoordinator/AgentSession-bound state) leaves composition, cancelling any coroutine launched from it. On iOS, RootView.swift wraps ChatHomeView/AgentView/SettingsView in a SwiftUI `TabView` (RootView.swift:21-33), which keeps every tab's view (and its Task-launched work) alive across tab switches — so the equivalent iOS `Task { await model.send(prompt) }` / `Task { await session.run(goal: g) }` is never cancelled by navigation. This is a genuine one-platform-missing-what-the-other-has parity gap, not a documented tradeoff.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Either (a) hoist the ChatModel/ConversationCoordinator and AgentSession instances above MainTabs (e.g. into AppRoot, same lifetime as `engine`) so their coroutine scope survives tab switches, mirroring iOS's app-level @StateObject ownership, or (b) replace the single-slot `when (tab)` with a pattern that keeps all three tab subtrees composed (e.g. `Box` with `visibleIf`/alpha instead of conditional composition), or (c) launch send/run on `viewModelScope`/an application-scoped CoroutineScope instead of `rememberCoroutineScope()`.
confirmed.failureScenario: Tap Send in Chat, immediately tap the Agent or Settings tab, wait, tap back to Chat: the assistant's reply that was being generated is gone (coroutine was cancelled mid-`chat.send`), and if `busy`/`running` was never reset before cancellation, the UI's in-flight visual state is also lost/inconsistent with iOS behavior where the same navigation does not interrupt the generation.
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed by reading MainTabs.kt, ChatScreen.kt, AgentScreen.kt, and RootView.swift. MainTabs.kt:63-68 uses `when (tab) { 0 -> ChatScreen(...); 1 -> AgentScreen(...); else -> SettingsScreen(...) }` inside a Box — Compose's `when` branching means only the selected branch is in composition; switching tabs removes the other screen's composable entirely from the tree. ChatScreen.kt:76 (`val coordinator = remember { ConversationCoordinator(ChatModel(engine), persistence) }`) and AgentScreen.kt:67 (`val session = remember { AgentSession(engine, tools).apply {...} }`) both scope their state/session objects to `remember` inside the screen composable itself (not hoisted to MainTabs or passed in), and both screens launch in-flight work via `rememberCoroutineScope()` (ChatScreen.kt:67, AgentScreen.kt:61), whose scope is cancelled when the composable leaves composition. So tapping away mid-generation cancels the coroutine and destroys the coordinator/session (losing any state not yet flushed to `chat.messages`/persistence). By contrast, RootView.swift:18-37 shows the iOS TabView contains all three tab views as siblings in a single Group/TabView — `onboarding`, `conversations`, and `agent` are all owned by RootView's own properties (passed in from the app target as StateObjects per the file's doc comment), not re-created or torn down per tab; SwiftUI's TabView keeps inactive tabs' view hierarchies (and their bound ObservableObjects, which live above the view layer anyway) alive. This is a genuine, reproducible cross-platform parity gap: tap Send in Chat, immediately switch tabs, wait for the reply, switch back — on Android the coroutine doing `chat.send(text)` was cancelled and a new ChatScreen/coordinator is created from scratch (re-restoring only what coordinator.persist() had already flushed), so the in-flight assistant reply is lost; on iOS the same sequence lets model.send(prompt) continue running against the same ChatModel instance regardless of which tab is frontmost. Draft text loss is also real since `input`/`goal` are also scoped via local `remember` inside the torn-down composable.
confirmed.reproduction: 1. On Android, open Chat tab, type a message, tap Send (busy=true, scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO){ chat.send(text) } starts). 2. Immediately tap the Agent or Settings bottom-nav item before the send completes. 3. MainTabs.kt's `when` removes ChatScreen from composition; rememberCoroutineScope() tied to that composition is cancelled, cancelling the chat.send(text) coroutine mid-flight; the local `coordinator`/`chat` created via `remember` in ChatScreen.kt:76 is discarded. 4. Tap back to the Chat tab: ChatScreen recomposes from scratch, creating a brand-new ConversationCoordinator/ChatModel via remember, restoring only whatever was persisted (finally-block's coordinator.persist() never ran because the coroutine was cancelled before reaching the finally block, or ran with an incomplete assistant turn) -- the assistant's answer never appears. Compare to iOS: RootView.swift keeps ChatHomeView/AgentView/SettingsView as permanent TabView children bound to the same long-lived ConversationCoordinator/AgentSession passed into RootView's initializer, so switching tabs does not cancel in-flight `model.send()`/agent Task work.
confirmed.change: In MainTabs.kt, replaced the single-slot `when (tab) { 0 -> ChatScreen(...); 1 -> AgentScreen(...); else -> SettingsScreen(...) }` (which tore down the non-active tab's composable subtree, cancelling any rememberCoroutineScope()-launched coroutine in it) with three always-composed Box layers, one per tab, each wrapped with a new private `Modifier.tabVisibility(visible: Boolean)` extension. The active tab gets alpha=1/zIndex=1; inactive tabs get alpha=0/zIndex=0, are removed from the accessibility tree via clearAndSetSemantics, and have their touch input swallowed via an indication-less `clickable(enabled = !visible) {}` so they can't be tapped through. This keeps ChatScreen's and AgentScreen's composition (and thus their rememberCoroutineScope()-launched send/run coroutines) alive across tab switches, matching iOS RootView's TabView behavior, without touching AgentScreen.kt/ChatScreen.kt or hoisting state outside MainTabs.kt (which was out of scope).
confirmed.verified: Full Gradle/Android-Studio build isn't available in this environment (the file's own doc-comment says 'App/cliff layer — build in Android Studio'), so I verified by: (1) re-reading the complete edited file end-to-end to confirm imports resolve to real Compose APIs (androidx.compose.ui.draw.alpha, androidx.compose.ui.zIndex, androidx.compose.foundation.clickable, androidx.compose.foundation.interaction.MutableInteractionSource — all standard Compose Foundation/UI APIs used correctly with matching signatures), (2) confirming brace/paren balance (24/24 braces, 46/46 parens), and (3) confirming the `@Composable fun Modifier.tabVisibility()` extension is only invoked inline inside the `MainTabs` composable body (valid Compose call-site for a composable Modifier extension that itself calls `remember`).

confirmed.title: Android's production DownloadStore is never persisted across WorkManager worker runs, defeating its documented purpose
confirmed.symptom: `ModelDownloadWorker.doWork()` constructs `DownloadStore()` with the default empty `initial` list every time WorkManager re-runs the worker (after process death, retry, or resume), and never wires `onChange` to persist the snapshot anywhere. Any UI or diagnostic code that queries `DownloadStore.resumable()`, `.get(modelId)`, or `.all()` for progress-after-relaunch will see an empty/wrong table, even though the underlying `.part` file resume (via `sink.existingSize(tempPath)` in ModelDownloadEngine.kt:94) works correctly and is unaffected.
confirmed.rootCause: DownloadStore.kt's own doc comment (lines 4-5, 27-31) states its purpose is 'so progress survives the app being suspended or killed' and that 'the app persists [snapshot] ... and restores via the constructor.' iOS's DownloadStore.swift is a file-backed actor (`init(fileURL:)` loads existing records from disk at construction, `persist()` writes to disk on every mutation) — the actual persistence contract the Kotlin doc comment promises. The Android production wiring in ModelDownloadWorker.kt never implements either half of that contract: `DownloadStore()` is always constructed with the empty default, and `onChange` (DownloadStore.kt:37) is never assigned to a persistence callback anywhere in the app module (confirmed via grep — no `store.onChange = ` assignment exists in android/app or android/quenderin-core outside of DownloadStore.kt itself).
confirmed.suggestedFix: In ModelDownloadWorker.doWork(), load an initial DownloadStore snapshot from a persisted file/SharedPreferences (mirroring iOS's fileURL-backed actor init) and wire `store.onChange = { persistSnapshot(it) }` to write it back out, so DownloadStore actually fulfills its documented cross-relaunch contract on Android the way it does on iOS.
confirmed.failureScenario: Any Android UI code added later that calls `DownloadStore.resumable()` or `.get(modelId)` to show 'resuming previous download' state after an app relaunch will always get an empty table / null record, even while a WorkManager-managed download is genuinely mid-flight and correctly resuming its bytes on disk — the observability layer silently desyncs from the real download state.
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed by direct read. DownloadStore.kt:29-31 documents the app is responsible for persisting `snapshot()` and restoring via the constructor's `initial` param — mirroring iOS's file-backed actor. ModelDownloadWorker.kt:50 constructs `DownloadStore()` with the default empty list every doWork() invocation (which WorkManager re-triggers after process death/retry) and never sets `onChange`, so the snapshot is never written anywhere and never restored. Grep across android/ confirms ModelDownloadWorker.kt is the only production site constructing DownloadStore besides ModelDownloadEngine's own constructor parameter, and no production code currently calls `.resumable()`/`.get()`/`.all()` for UI purposes — so today this is latent/dead rather than actively desyncing a visible UI. However, it is a genuine, reproducible contract violation: the class's own doc comment states its persistence purpose, WorkManagerModelDownloader.kt never touches DownloadStore either (it relies solely on WorkInfo polling, unrelated to DownloadStore), and any future caller of DownloadStore.resumable()/get() (which the iOS twin already exposes via BackgroundModelDownloader.resumableDownloads(), suggesting the Android equivalent is expected) would silently get empty/stale data despite the .part file resume genuinely working via ModelDownloadEngine.kt's own existingSize() check. This matches the specified cross-platform parity bug class exactly (a bookkeeping structure present on Android that mirrors iOS's but isn't wired to actually persist).
confirmed.reproduction: 1. WorkManagerModelDownloader.download() enqueues a WorkManager job; ModelDownloadWorker.doWork() runs, constructing `DownloadStore()` fresh (empty), download proceeds and mutates that in-memory store's records via ModelDownloadEngine — but nothing persists this to disk. 2. Process is killed (OOM, user swipe-kill) mid-download. 3. WorkManager reschedules the worker; doWork() runs again, constructing yet another fresh empty `DownloadStore()` — again with no `initial` records restored from step 1's state (which was never written anywhere). 4. Underlying `.part` file resume still works correctly (ModelDownloadEngine.kt:94, `sink.existingSize(tempPath)` reads directly from the filesystem, independent of DownloadStore). But any code that queries the DownloadStore instance for resumable/in-flight state (as the iOS twin's `resumableDownloads()` is designed to support) sees nothing — the store is not the source of truth it claims to be.
confirmed.change: In ModelDownloadWorker.kt doWork(), replaced the always-empty `DownloadStore()` with a store constructed from a snapshot loaded off disk (`loadSnapshot(storeFile)`), and wired `store.onChange = { saveSnapshot(storeFile, it) }` so every mutation is persisted back out. Added two small private helpers, loadSnapshot/saveSnapshot, using a dependency-free tab-delimited text format (no JSON library exists in this module — confirmed via grep) written to a `download_store.txt` file in `applicationContext.filesDir`, mirroring the fileURL-backed persistence contract iOS's actor-based DownloadStore.swift already implements and that DownloadStore.kt's own doc comment promises ('the app persists snapshot ... and restores via the constructor').
confirmed.verified: ModelDownloadWorker.kt itself requires the Android SDK/AGP (per its own doc comment, 'REQUIRES the Android SDK + AGP to build ... intentionally NOT part of the headless kotlinc check'), so it is out of scope for a standalone kotlinc build; no Gradle/AGP toolchain is available in this environment to compile it directly. Instead verified the change doesn't touch or break :quenderin-core (only imports the unmodified PersistedDownload type from it): ran `cd android/quenderin-core && kotlinc src/main/kotlin/ai/quenderin/core/*.kt src/verify/CoreVerify.kt -include-runtime -d /tmp/core_verify_download.jar` (compiled cleanly, zero errors) followed by `java -jar /tmp/core_verify_download.jar`, which printed 'ALL PASSED' with zero FAIL lines across all checks including the DownloadStore/PersistedDownload and ModelDownloadEngine suites. Also manually traced the Kotlin syntax (PersistedDownload field order/types, PersistedDownload.State enum values RUNNING/PAUSED/COMPLETED/FAILED) against DownloadStore.kt to confirm correctness.

confirmed.title: iOS applies an in-flight thermal governor that re-tunes threads mid-generation; Android has an equivalent ThermalGovernor class but never wires it into the native decode loop
confirmed.symptom: On a long Android generation, once threads are set at load time (nativeLoad, llama_jni.cpp:165-166), they are never adjusted again even if the device heats up mid-reply. On iOS, `runGeneration` samples `ThermalMonitor.currentLevel()` every 32 tokens and calls `llama_set_n_threads` to shed threads as the SoC heats (LlamaEngine.swift:248-260), so a long iOS generation self-throttles to stay thermally sustainable while the equivalent Android generation does not.
confirmed.rootCause: Android's `ThermalGovernor` class exists (ThermalMonitor.kt:52, explicitly documented as "twin of iOS ThermalGovernor") but is never referenced anywhere in the native decode path: not in `llama_jni.cpp`, not in the shared `generateWithKVReuse` loop in `llama_generate.h`, and not in `LlamaEngine.kt`'s `complete()`/`complete(prompt, onToken)` (confirmed via grep — zero hits for ThermalGovernor/llama_set_n_threads across all three files). Android only applies thermal-aware thread selection ONCE, at load time, via `ThermalThrottle.recommendedThreads(thermalLevel, base)` in `LlamaEngine.kt:77`, using whatever `thermalLevel` was cached before the generation started. There is no llama.cpp `llama_set_n_threads` equivalent call anywhere in the JNI bridge or generation loop, and the per-token loop in llama_generate.h:99-134 has no periodic thermal check at all.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Wire the existing Kotlin `ThermalGovernor` into the generation path the same way iOS does: sample the current thermal level periodically (e.g. every 32 tokens) inside the JNI generate loop (or via a callback into Kotlin passed through the `emit`/`cancelled` lambda pattern already used for cancellation), and call `llama_set_n_threads(ctx, n, n)` when the governor recommends a change, mirroring LlamaEngine.swift:257-260 and threading the call through `generateWithKVReuse`'s template parameters similar to how `cancelled()` is already threaded through.
confirmed.failureScenario: A user on Android runs a long, multi-hundred-token generation on a device that starts cool. The phone heats up over the course of the reply (as documented as the exact target failure mode this project cares about — see ThreadPlanner.kt's and ThermalMonitor's own comments about heat being "the sustained-load ceiling on a phone"). On iOS the same scenario sheds threads mid-generation and sustains; on Android threads stay pinned at the load-time count for the whole generation, risking sustained thermal throttling or a slower/hotter tail of the reply that iOS would have avoided — an already-partially-built fix (the Kotlin class exists) that was never connected to the actual decode loop.
confirmed.reasoning: Read the full llama_jni.cpp (all JNI exports: nativeLoad, nativeComplete, nativeCompleteStreaming, nativeFree, the private generate() helper) and the full generateWithKVReuse loop in llama_generate.h: there is no call to llama_set_n_threads anywhere, no thermal sampling, and no ThermalGovernor usage in the native decode loop. nativeLoad sets cp.n_threads/n_threads_batch once at context-creation time (lines 165-166) and it is never revisited. Grepping the Kotlin LlamaEngine.kt/InferenceEngine.kt for ThermalGovernor, nativeSetThreads, or llama_set_n_threads also found nothing — there isn't even a JNI export through which Kotlin could invoke a mid-decode thread change, so the Kotlin ThermalGovernor class (ThermalMonitor.kt) is unreachable dead code with respect to actual native decode. Notably, ThermalMonitor.kt:48-50 contains a comment explicitly claiming 'On Android the in-decode sampling + llama_set_n_threads call live in the JNI C++ loop' — this claim is false/stale against the actual llama_jni.cpp/llama_generate.h contents, which strengthens rather than refutes the finding (it shows the intended wiring was documented but never implemented, or was later ripped out without updating the comment). Meanwhile iOS's runGeneration (LlamaEngine.swift:248-260) genuinely samples ThermalMonitor.currentLevel() every 32 tokens via a ThermalGovernor instance and calls llama_set_n_threads on change. This is exactly the cross-platform parity-gap bug class called out in scope.
confirmed.reproduction: Start a long (multi-hundred-token) generation on Android on a device that heats up mid-reply. Because n_threads is fixed at load time in nativeLoad (llama_jni.cpp:165-166) and never revisited in generateWithKVReuse's per-token loop (llama_generate.h:99-134), the thread count never sheds despite the device thermally throttling, unlike iOS's runGeneration which re-tunes every 32 tokens via ThermalGovernor.update() + llama_set_n_threads (LlamaEngine.swift:251-260).
confirmed.change:
Wired an in-flight thermal governor into the Android native decode loop, mirroring iOS's periodic re-sampling (previously Android only applied thermal-aware threads once, at nativeLoad):
1. android/jni/llama_generate.h: added an optional `ThermalPoll` template parameter (default no-op lambda, so existing callers like the smoke test are unaffected) to `generateWithKVReuse`. Inside the per-token loop, every 32 tokens (`kThermalSampleInterval`) it calls `thermalPoll()` and applies `llama_set_n_threads(ctx, n, n)` when it returns a positive count. Updated the function's doc comment to document the new `thermalPoll()` parameter contract.
2. android/jni/llama_jni.cpp: in `generate()`, resolved a new `recommendedThreads()` jmethodID on `thiz` (mirroring how `cancelRequested`'s jfieldID is already resolved), and built a `thermalPoll` lambda that calls back into Kotlin via `CallIntMethod`, only returning a non-zero value when the recommendation actually changes from the last-applied count (avoiding a redundant `llama_set_n_threads` call every 32 tokens). Passed this lambda as the new trailing argument to `generateWithKVReuse`.
3. android/quenderin-core/.../LlamaEngine.kt: added a `@Volatile private var loadedBaseThreads: Int = 1` field (mirrors Swift's `loadedBaseThreads`), set it in `load()` alongside the existing `base` computation, and added a public `fun recommendedThreads(): Int = ThermalThrottle.recommendedThreads(thermalLevel, loadedBaseThreads)` method that the native side polls — reusing the already-in-scope `ThermalThrottle`/`ThermalLevel` symbols already referenced by this file's `load()`, without touching the out-of-scope ThermalMonitor.kt.
4. android/quenderin-core/src/verify/CoreVerify.kt: added one check in the existing 'LlamaEngine: fails cleanly off-device' section pinning that `recommendedThreads()` is callable and degrades safely (floor of 1 thread) when queried at NOMINAL vs CRITICAL thermal levels pre-load.
confirmed.verified: kotlinc build: `cd android/quenderin-core && kotlinc src/main/kotlin/ai/quenderin/core/*.kt src/verify/CoreVerify.kt -include-runtime -d /tmp/core_verify_inference.jar` completed with zero compiler errors/warnings. `java -jar /tmp/core_verify_inference.jar` printed 'ALL PASSED' with zero FAIL lines, including 'ok   LlamaEngine.recommendedThreads reflects live thermalLevel against the load-time baseline'. For the C++ side (llama_generate.h, llama_jni.cpp), no NDK build is available in this environment — re-read both edited regions in full afterward: the new `ThermalPoll` template parameter with a matching default lambda type (`int(*)()` default type + `[]()->int{return 0;}` default value) is valid C++17 and only affects callers that omit the trailing args; the JNI call site passes an explicit capturing lambda (deduced independently of the default), `GetMethodID`/`CallIntMethod` usage matches the file's existing `cancel_fid`/`GetBooleanField` pattern exactly (same null-safety idiom via short-circuit `&&`/early-return). No new headers, no ABI/signature changes to existing exported JNI functions, and CMakeLists.txt required no changes since no new source files or link dependencies were introduced.

confirmed.title: ConversationCoordinator.open()/startNew() can persist a mid-stream placeholder/partial assistant message (iOS-only risk; Android's synchronous send() cannot expose this state)
confirmed.symptom: Calling coordinator.open(id:) or coordinator.startNew() while a reply is still streaming (ChatModel.isGenerating == true) saves the conversation being left with a trailing assistant message that is empty ("") or a truncated partial string, instead of either the complete reply or no assistant turn at all.
confirmed.rootCause: ChatModel.swift's send() (lines 47-52) synchronously appends the user message AND a placeholder empty-text assistant ChatMessage to `messages` before any `await` for the stream; that placeholder is then mutated in place token-by-token. ConversationCoordinator.persist() (line 38-42), open() (line 55-59) and startNew() (line 46-52) only guard on `chat.messages.isEmpty` — never on `chat.isGenerating` — so if a caller navigates away/opens another conversation while a reply is in flight, `persist()` writes `chat.messages` (which includes the half-built assistant message) to disk via manager.save(). On Android, ChatModel.kt's send() (ChatModel.kt:38-49) is fully synchronous and only appends the assistant ChatMessage after `engine.complete()` returns the full reply (line 46), so `chat.messages` can never contain a partial/placeholder assistant turn when ConversationCoordinator.kt's persist()/open()/startNew() run — this exact failure mode is structurally impossible on Android, making this a cross-platform parity gap in addition to a plain bug.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Guard persist()/startNew()/open()/delete() in ConversationCoordinator.swift on `!chat.isGenerating` (or otherwise skip persisting a transcript whose last message is an empty/in-progress assistant turn), matching the invariant Android gets for free from its synchronous send().
confirmed.failureScenario: User sends a prompt, immediately taps 'New Conversation' (or opens a different history item) before the stream token loop appends any tokens; coordinator.startNew()/open() calls persist() which writes a transcript ending in ChatMessage(role: .assistant, text: "") to disk for the abandoned conversation. Re-opening that conversation later shows a dangling empty assistant bubble, and re-exporting via ConversationExporter renders a '**Quenderin:**\n\n' block with no content.
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed by direct trace. ChatModel.swift send() (lines 43-73) is async: it appends an empty-text assistant placeholder (line 51-52) BEFORE any tokens arrive, then awaits engine.generate() (line 59) and each token in the stream (line 60) -- both are actor-yield points. ConversationCoordinator.persist() (line 38-42) has no isGenerating guard and simply writes chat.messages verbatim via manager.save(). startNew() (line 46-52) and open() (line 55-59) both call persist() unconditionally before mutating/replacing the chat. So if the UI invokes startNew()/open() while isGenerating==true and before the first token lands, persist() serializes a transcript ending in ChatMessage(role: .assistant, text: "") to disk. ConversationExporter.swift (lines 13-16) then renders '**Quenderin:**\n\n' with no body for that message, exactly as described. Verified the Android counterpart is immune: ChatModel.kt send() (lines 38-49) is fully synchronous -- it only appends the assistant message (line 46) after engine.complete() (line 45) fully returns, so _messages never contains a partial placeholder observable by a concurrent coordinator call. This makes it a genuine iOS-only parity/correctness gap, not a hypothetical -- no guard anywhere in ConversationCoordinator.swift or ChatModel.swift prevents startNew()/open() from firing mid-stream, and no code strips trailing empty assistant messages before persist.
confirmed.reproduction: 1) User taps send; ChatModel.send() appends user msg + empty assistant placeholder (text: ""), sets isGenerating=true, awaits engine.generate(). 2) Before any token arrives, user taps 'New Conversation'; coordinator.startNew() runs on the same MainActor task queue during the suspension, chat.messages is non-empty so it proceeds, calls persist() which manager.save()s the transcript including the trailing empty-text assistant message. 3) manager.startNew() + chat.reset() then abandon that conversation, but its on-disk file already has the dangling '' assistant turn. Reopening it later or exporting via ConversationExporter shows the empty/truncated bubble.
confirmed.change: In apple/QuenderinKit/Sources/QuenderinKit/ConversationCoordinator.swift, added a `!chat.isGenerating` guard to `persist()` (line ~38-42), alongside the existing `manager.currentID`/`!chat.messages.isEmpty` guards, plus a comment explaining why: `chat.messages` ends in a placeholder/partial assistant turn until `send()` completes, and `startNew()`/`open()` both route through `persist()`, so guarding this single choke point stops a mid-stream transcript (trailing empty/partial assistant message) from ever being written to disk. `delete()`'s fallback path calls `manager.open()`/`manager.startNew()` directly, not `persist()`, so it was already unaffected by this invariant and needed no change.
confirmed.verified: swift build succeeds cleanly. Added regression test `testPersistIsNoOpWhileGenerating` in ConversationCoordinatorTests.swift using the same isGenerating spin-wait pattern already used in ChatModelTests (`while chat.messages.count < 2 { await Task.yield() }`). Confirmed the test FAILS against the pre-fix code (XCTAssertEqual failed: 'hello' != 'New conversation' — proving the mid-stream title/content leaked to the persisted summary) and PASSES against the fixed code. Full conversation-subsystem test run: `swift test --filter ConversationCoordinatorTests|ConversationStoreTests|ConversationManagerTests|ChatModelTests|ConversationExporterTests|ConversationLibraryTests|ConversationContextTests` -> 43 tests, 0 failures. This change is Apple-only (Kotlin ChatModel.kt/ConversationCoordinator.kt are structurally immune per the bug report, and were not modified), so no android/quenderin-core CoreVerify.kt update or kotlinc build was needed.

confirmed.title: AgentScreen Run button missing the synchronous busy-flag guard that ChatScreen explicitly added to close the exact same double-tap race
confirmed.symptom: Rapidly double-tapping "Run" on the Agent screen can enqueue two concurrent `session.run(g)` calls before `running` flips to `true`, whereas the analogous double-tap on the Chat screen's Send button is explicitly prevented.
confirmed.rootCause: In ChatScreen.kt (lines 150-168), the developer set `busy = true` synchronously in the onClick lambda, on the main thread, before dispatching `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { chat.send(text) }` — the code comment explicitly says this is done "so a rapid double-tap can't enqueue a second send before IO flips the flag." AgentScreen.kt's Run button (lines 166-173) does not set `running = true` locally; `running` is only ever updated via the `onChange` callback wired at AgentScreen.kt:72 (`running = this.isRunning`), which fires from inside `AgentSession` after `session.run(g)` actually executes on the `Dispatchers.IO` thread (line 171). Between the click and that coroutine actually running (thread-pool dispatch latency), the Button stays `enabled = !running && goal.isNotBlank()` = true, so a second rapid tap can call `session.run(g)` again before the first call's `isRunning = true` has propagated back to Compose state.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Mirror ChatScreen's fix: set a local `running = true` synchronously in the onClick handler immediately before `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { session.run(g) }`, the same way `busy = true` is set in ChatScreen.kt line 157.
confirmed.failureScenario: User double-taps "Run" quickly with a valid goal typed: both taps pass the `enabled = !running && goal.isNotBlank()` check before the first `session.run()` call has set `isRunning`/`running` to true, so `AgentSession.run()` can be invoked twice concurrently, corrupting or duplicating the step transcript / racing on `steps`/`answer` state depending on AgentSession's internal handling of concurrent `run()` calls.
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed by direct comparison of AgentScreen.kt:166-173 and ChatScreen.kt:150-168 (both fully read). ChatScreen's Button onClick sets `busy = true` synchronously on the calling/main thread (line 157, with an explicit comment: 'Set busy synchronously on the calling (main) thread so a rapid double-tap can't enqueue a second send before IO flips the flag') BEFORE `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { chat.send(text) }` is invoked -- so the `enabled = !busy && ...` gate is correct for the very next recomposition triggered by that same click. AgentScreen's Button onClick (lines 168-172) does the opposite: it clears `goal` and calls `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { session.run(g) }` directly, with no local `running = true` set before the launch. `running` is only updated via the session's `onChange` callback (line 72: `running = this.isRunning`), which fires from inside `session.run()` -- code that executes on the Dispatchers.IO coroutine, meaning the `running` Compose state var is only mutated after that coroutine actually starts executing AND the callback's write is observed by Compose (typically requiring a hop back through Compose's snapshot state system). In the window between the first tap's `onClick` firing and that state actually landing, `enabled = !running && goal.isNotBlank()` still evaluates true for a second tap event delivered in the same input burst, so two `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { session.run(g) } ` calls can be dispatched before either coroutine has actually started running and set isRunning. Nothing else in AgentScreen.kt (no local var, no lock, no debounce) guards this -- the file has zero synchronous state set at click time, unlike ChatScreen's explicit, commented fix for the identical pattern. This is exactly the bug class ChatScreen.kt's own comment documents fixing, present unaddressed in AgentScreen.kt.
confirmed.reproduction: 1. On the Agent screen, type a valid goal so `goal.isNotBlank()` is true and `running` is false. 2. Rapidly double-tap the 'Run' button (two down/up events delivered within the same or consecutive frames, e.g. via fast synthetic taps or fat-finger double contact). 3. First tap's onClick executes: `goal = ""`, then `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { session.run(g) }` is dispatched to a background dispatcher -- this returns immediately without blocking or mutating `running`. 4. Second tap's onClick fires before Compose recomposes with `running = true` (which only happens after `session.run()` starts executing on IO and its onChange callback's `running = this.isRunning` write is picked up) -- `enabled` still reads `!running (true) && goal.isNotBlank()`; but `goal` was already cleared to "" by the first tap, so in practice a rapid double tap right after typing (both taps queued before the first launch's isRunning=true propagates) can still race, and more clearly: two legitimate back-to-back Run presses on different goals typed in quick succession before the internal isRunning flip is observed can concurrently invoke session.run(), unlike ChatScreen where busy=true is guaranteed set before the second click's enabled check runs.
confirmed.change: In AgentScreen.kt's Run button onClick handler (originally lines 166-173), added `running = true` synchronously on the main thread immediately before `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { session.run(g) }`, with a comment mirroring ChatScreen.kt's existing busy-flag comment verbatim in spirit ('so a rapid double-tap can't enqueue a second run before IO flips the flag'). This closes the same race ChatScreen.kt already closes for Send: `enabled = !running && goal.isNotBlank()` now sees running=true immediately after the first tap, so a second rapid tap is blocked before session.run() can be invoked twice concurrently.
confirmed.verified: Same environment constraint as bug 0 (app module needs Android Studio/Gradle to build, not available standalone here). Verified by re-reading the edited region (lines 166-177): the change is a single added line plus a two-line comment, directly parallel to ChatScreen.kt's existing `busy = true` guard at ChatScreen.kt:157, using the same `running` MutableState already declared and wired via `onChange` at line 64/72 — no new symbols, no signature changes. Brace/paren balance for the full file confirmed (57/57 braces, 104/104 parens).

confirmed.title: ConversationStore.decode() diverges cross-platform on an unrecognized role byte: Android drops the message, iOS relabels it as assistant
confirmed.symptom: When a persisted transcript's role token is corrupted/unrecognized, Android silently removes that one message from the restored conversation, while iOS silently reinterprets it as an assistant message (keeping it, but with the wrong speaker) — same trigger, different data-loss/corruption shape between platforms.
confirmed.rootCause: Android's ConversationStore.kt:22-23 uses `Role.valueOf(...)` wrapped in `runCatching{}.getOrNull() ?: return@mapNotNull null`, so an unparseable role causes `mapNotNull` to drop that line entirely. iOS's ConversationStore.swift:36 uses `ChatMessage.Role(rawValue: role) ?? .assistant`, silently coercing any unrecognized role string to `.assistant` and keeping the message (with its original text) mislabeled.
confirmed.suggestedFix: Pick one recovery strategy and mirror it on both platforms — either drop unparseable rows everywhere (matches Android and avoids feeding a spoofed 'Assistant:' line back into ConversationContext.build() on next send()), or keep-but-flag on both.
confirmed.failureScenario: A transcript file is partially corrupted (disk bit-rot, manual edit, or a future format migration bug) such that one row's role token no longer matches 'USER'/'user' or 'ASSISTANT'/'assistant'. On iOS, ConversationManager.open() returns that row as an assistant message; when the user sends a new prompt, ConversationContext.build() (ConversationContext.swift) replays it to the model prefixed 'Assistant:' even though the original author may have been the user, changing what the model conditions on. On Android the same row is simply dropped, changing message count/order instead. Both are silent, non-crashing, but produce different restored transcripts from the same corrupted input.
confirmed.reasoning: Confirmed by direct comparison of the two decode paths. Kotlin ConversationStore.kt decode() (lines 17-26): 'val role = runCatching { Role.valueOf(line.substring(0, tab)) }.getOrNull() ?: return@mapNotNull null' -- an unrecognized role token causes the entire line to be dropped via mapNotNull. Swift ConversationStore.swift line 36: 'ChatMessage(role: ChatMessage.Role(rawValue: role) ?? .assistant, text: text)' -- an unrecognized role token silently coerces to .assistant instead of being dropped. Same corrupted input (a role byte that matches neither USER/user nor ASSISTANT/assistant) produces different restored transcripts: Android loses a message (count/order changes), iOS keeps it but mislabels the speaker. Verified downstream impact: ConversationContext.swift build() (lines 63-66) uses message.role to choose the 'User:'/'Assistant:' prefix when replaying history to the model, so a message that was actually authored by the user but relabeled .assistant on decode does get fed back to the engine under the wrong speaker tag, changing what the model conditions on for future turns. No validation or guard elsewhere in either file corrects this -- it is an unhandled, silent divergence, not a documented tradeoff (the code comments describe the wire format rationale but do not address malformed-role recovery on either platform, and the two platforms disagree on the recovery strategy).
confirmed.reproduction: 1) A transcript's persisted role token is corrupted (e.g. disk bit-rot flips a character, or a future migration bug leaves a stray/garbled role string) so it matches neither 'USER'/'user' nor 'ASSISTANT'/'assistant'/'ASSISTANT'. 2) On Kotlin: ConversationStore.decode() drops that row -- restored conversation has one fewer message, and subsequent messages shift position/adjacency. 3) On Swift: ConversationStore.decode() keeps the row but as ChatMessage(role: .assistant, ...) -- restored conversation has the same message count but with a mislabeled speaker; if the original author was actually the user, ConversationContext.build() will now prefix it 'Assistant:' when replaying to the model on the next turn, changing the prompt fed to the engine relative to what Android reconstructs from the same file.
confirmed.change: In apple/QuenderinKit/Sources/QuenderinKit/ConversationStore.swift, changed `StoredMessage.chatMessage` from a non-optional computed property that coerced an unparseable role to `.assistant` (`ChatMessage.Role(rawValue: role) ?? .assistant`) into an optional (`ChatMessage.Role(rawValue: role).map { ChatMessage(role: $0, text: text) }`), and changed `decode()` from `.map(\.chatMessage)` to `.compactMap(\.chatMessage)` so a row with an unparseable role is dropped instead of kept and mislabeled — mirroring Kotlin ConversationStore.kt's `mapNotNull` drop-on-unparseable-role behavior. Added a WHY comment: coercing would replay a spoofed 'Assistant:' line into ConversationContext.build() on the next turn.
confirmed.verified: swift build succeeds cleanly. Added regression test `testUnparseableRoleRowIsDroppedNotCoerced` in ConversationStoreTests.swift that encodes a message, corrupts its role field to an unrecognized string, decodes it, and asserts the result is empty (row dropped). Confirmed the test FAILS against the pre-fix code (XCTAssertTrue failed — the corrupted row was kept, coerced to .assistant) and PASSES against the fixed code. Full conversation-subsystem test run: 43 tests, 0 failures, including all 5 ConversationStoreTests. Apple-only change (Kotlin ConversationStore.kt already implements the drop behavior and was not modified), so no android/quenderin-core changes or kotlinc build was needed.
